title: He Kōrero o Te Awa: Voices of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream | Year 7 English: Analysing Oral Histories and Place-Based Narratives
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream) through Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau (traditional stories), historical accounts of local flax mills that operated along its banks in the late 1800s, and contemporary voices advocating for its restoration. This waterway physically borders our college and is a living text connecting past, present, and future.
learningObjectives: Analyse and compare different text types (oral pūrākau, historical record, persuasive writing) to identify how language features are used to convey perspectives on place.
Synthesise information from a local oral history interview and written sources to create a short, fact-based narrative about the awa.
Use descriptive language and imagery inspired by the local environment to write a poem or short piece that expresses a personal connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Participate actively and respectfully in a whakarongo (listening) session with a local kaikōrero (speaker), formulating relevant questions about their connection to the awa.

activities.title: He Whakarongo: Listening to the Awa
activities.description: Students will sit beside Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku for 5 minutes of silent observation and listening. They will use a 'senses map' graphic organiser to record what they see, hear, and feel. This will be followed by a korero about initial impressions and the concept of a place having a voice or a story.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero Tuku Iho: Stories Passed Down
activities.description: In small groups, students will analyse two texts: a written version of a Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau related to local waterways and a primary source excerpt from a 19th-century diary describing the Mangakōtukutuku flax mills. Using a Venn diagram, they will compare the purpose, audience, and language features of each text.

activities.title: Te Hīkoi: A Walk with a Kaikōrero
activities.description: The class will be joined by a local kaumātua or representative from a local environmental group (e.g., Nourish Hawke's Bay) for a guided walk along a section of the awa. The focus will be on listening to their stories and perspectives. Students will prepare questions beforehand to learn about historical and current relationships with the stream.

activities.title: Taku Kōrero: My Voice for the Awa
activities.description: Using their notes from the previous activities as inspiration, students will craft a piece of creative writing. They can choose to write a poem, a short narrative, or a persuasive paragraph from the perspective of the awa itself or someone connected to it, using descriptive and emotive language.

resources: Local Kaikōrero: A kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu or a representative from Nourish Hawke's Bay / Enviroschools.
Digital Archive: Papers Past website (paperspast.natlib.govt.nz) for historical newspaper articles on the Mangakōtukutuku flax mills.
Text: 'Ngā Pūrākau o Ngāti Kahungunu' by Dr. Moana Jackson or similar resource from the school library/local iwi archives.
Tool: 'Senses Map' graphic organiser template and Venn diagram worksheet.

tasks: Completed 'Senses Map' from the initial observation activity.
Completed Venn diagram comparing the pūrākau and historical text.
A set of 3 thoughtful questions prepared for the kaikōrero.

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features.
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Purposes and audiences; Ideas; Language features.
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways.
Key Competencies: Participating and contributing; Relating to others.
